title: Importing Medicines from Third Countries: The QP's Role
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: QP importation third country certification explained: the QP's legal duties, importation testing, MRAs, supplier qualification and GDP for UK and EU importers.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, with a single subtle green accent light. Soft natural daylight rakes across a pristine quality-control bench where a gloved hand inspects a sealed vial beside neatly stacked batch documentation and a temperature-logged shipping container in the soft-focus background. Shallow depth of field draws the eye to the vial and a certificate of analysis, suggesting cross-border quality assurance and release. Composition is calm, premium and editorial, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos, no readable labels.

faqs.q: Does every batch imported from a third country need to be re-tested in the UK or EU?
faqs.a: As a default, yes: full qualitative and quantitative testing is expected on importation of finished products from third countries. This requirement can be waived only where a Mutual Recognition Agreement or equivalent arrangement genuinely covers the specific product and tests in question. The QP must confirm the scope of any such agreement for each product rather than assuming it applies.

faqs.q: Can a Qualified Person delegate responsibility for certifying imported batches?
faqs.a: No. Certification is a personal legal responsibility held by the named QP on the manufacturing/importation authorisation, and it cannot be transferred through a contract or purchase order. A QP may rely on the work of others, such as auditors and analysts, to build the evidence, but the certification decision and its accountability remain theirs alone. This is why a clear, documented decision trail is essential.

faqs.q: How do the QP and Responsible Person roles interact in importation?
faqs.a: The QP certifies that each imported batch meets its marketing authorisation and GMP, while the Responsible Person ensures the product is stored and distributed under Good Distribution Practice. The two roles are closely linked because transport and storage conditions during importation directly affect the certification decision. A defined interface in quality agreements and SOPs prevents accountability gaps between manufacturing release and wholesale distribution.
